CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T97 (goals:G4) — netns e2e proving 2+ edges to one concentrator stay isolated.
acceptance: go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e -run MultiPeer passes on both e2e hosts: independent per-edge inner streams; edge-A kill+restart leaves edge-B uninterrupted; per-peer /metrics attribution; existing single-peer e2e unchanged.
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-af9870e926cd7a191 (branch implement/T97, base a146fde, HEAD 6df7dbc). Gate via `nix develop -c sh -c '...'`. -tags e2e privileged tier NOT runnable here (deferred) — judge via compile/vet + inspection.
Worker: pass; test/e2e/multipeer_test.go; 2-edge multi-uplink topology; port 9102; spoofed flood via stdlib net.DialUDP.
Scrutinize adversarially: (a) run `go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go test ./...` yourself — all green? (b) Is the metrics port 9102 GENUINELY unique (grep all e2e *MetricsListen — T101 fixed 9099→9101, T104 has 9100; confirm 9102 is free)? (c) Is the per-peer /metrics attribution assertion CORRECT — does edge A really surface under peer="" and edge B under its configured name, per T93/T94's BoundPeerNames (primary forced to name="")? A wrong label expectation would make the hardware run fail spuriously. (d) Do the assertions follow the report-only discipline where absolute numbers apply (M10/Q12) but genuinely FAIL on the isolation properties (cross-edge corruption, edge-A-restart-disrupts-B, live-peer eviction)? (e) Is the spoofed-flood scenario (stdlib net.DialUDP from an unenrolled source) a faithful model of the threat, and does it assert no live-peer eviction (not just no crash)? (f) Are netns.go/thresholds.go genuinely untouched (existing e2e unaffected)? Flag what only the hardware run can validate. Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	//go:build e2e
2	
3	package e2e
4	
5	import (
6		"context"
7		"crypto/rand"
8		"encoding/json"
9		"fmt"
10		"net"
11		"net/http"
12		"os/exec"
13		"path/filepath"
14		"strconv"
15		"strings"
16		"testing"
17		"time"
18	
19		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
20	)
21	
22	// T97 is the privileged netns e2e for the G4 multi-peer concentrator: ONE concentrator
23	// terminates TWO edges, each edge bonded across its OWN pair of uplinks, and the test
24	// proves the peers stay ISOLATED end to end — the threat model of the multi-tenant hub:
25	//
26	//   - each edge's inner stream verifies INDEPENDENTLY (a bulk TCP transfer through each
27	//     tunnel completes; TCP's own integrity check means a cross-peer corruption would
28	//     surface as a reset / zero bytes, so two positive transfers prove no cross-talk);
29	//   - one edge's disruption (kill+restart) does NOT interrupt the other's live tunnel;
30	//   - return traffic routes to the CORRECT edge (the concentrator's WireGuard allowed_ips
31	//     crypto-routing sends each inner /32 back to its own peer);
32	//   - an edge NAT-rebind (source-address move on one uplink) recovers via the concentrator
33	//     re-learning the roamed endpoint from the next authenticated PROBE (T37), while the
34	//     other edge is undisturbed;
35	//   - a spoofed unbound-source UDP flood at the listen port degrades only bootstrap
36	//     latency (report-only, M10/Q12) WITHOUT evicting a live peer or corrupting a
37	//     cross-peer stream.
38	//
39	// It also scrapes the concentrator /metrics and asserts the T94 PER-PEER-labelled series
40	// attribute traffic to the correct edge for BOTH peers.
41	//
42	// EXECUTION IS DEFERRED (G2 pattern). The netns tier needs the privileged two-namespace
43	// fixture + CAP_NET_ADMIN and is NOT run in the unit environment; this file must COMPILE
44	// and vet under -tags e2e (`go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e` + `go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e`)
45	// and is executed on the o3.7mind.io (aarch64) + llm-ubuntu-0 (amd64) hosts.
46	//
47	// TOPOLOGY (why bridges). The concentrator runs in the BASE (test-process) network
48	// namespace — so its 127.0.0.1 /metrics endpoint is directly scrapeable by the test — and
49	// owns two uplink source addresses, one per emulated WAN /24. Each edge is its OWN network
50	// namespace (a PID-addressed holder, like the base fixture and hub_failover) with its own
51	// `wanbond0` TUN, bonded across two uplinks. Each WAN /24 is an L2 bridge in the base
52	// namespace carrying the concentrator's uplink address and BOTH edges' uplink on that WAN,
53	// so every edge reaches both concentrator uplinks without any routing:
54	//
55	//	base netns (concentrator, inner 10.10.0.2):
56	//	   bridge wbMp1 = 10.101.1.254/24 (conc uplink "wan1")   bridge wbMp2 = 10.101.2.254/24 (conc uplink "wan2")
57	//	        │                    │                                 │                    │
58	//	   edgeA wan1           edgeB wan1                         edgeA wan2           edgeB wan2
59	//	   10.101.1.1           10.101.1.2                         10.101.2.1           10.101.2.2
60	//	   ── edgeA netns (inner 10.10.0.1) ──                     ── edgeB netns (inner 10.10.0.3) ──
61	//
62	// The two uplinks carry DISTINCT netem delay (20ms vs 55ms) so a bonded edge's frames
63	// arrive out of order and exercise the connection-scoped resequencer independently per
64	// peer. Each edge's outer-control PSK (its top-level psk) equals that edge's per-peer psk
65	// in the concentrator config (T81: multi-peer requires present, pairwise-distinct per-peer
66	// psks), so the concentrator authenticates each edge's probe plane with the right key.
67	//
68	// PER-PEER METRIC LABEL. The concentrator's PRIMARY (first-configured) peer always binds
69	// UNNAMED — bind.Multipath.BoundPeerNames forces peers[0].name = "" on both a single-peer
70	// hub AND a multi-peer concentrator; only ADDITIONAL peers (AddConcentratorPeer) carry
71	// their configured name (device.go). So edge A, configured FIRST, surfaces under the
72	// `peer=""` label (NOT its config name "edge-alpha"), and edge B under `peer="edge-beta"`.
73	// The two labels are DISTINCT, which is all the attribution assertions need.
74	const (
75		// WAN-1 fabric: bridge + concentrator uplink address (the "wan1" path source_addr).
76		mpBr1     = "wbMp1"
77		mpConc1IP = "10.101.1.254"
78		mpConc1EP = mpConc1IP + ":51820"
79		// WAN-2 fabric.
80		mpBr2     = "wbMp2"
81		mpConc2IP = "10.101.2.254"
82		mpConc2EP = mpConc2IP + ":51820"
83	
84		// Edge A uplinks (bridge-port veth / in-namespace veth / address), one per WAN.
85		mpA1Port = "wbMpA1p"
86		mpA1Edge = "wbMpA1e"
87		mpA1IP   = "10.101.1.1"
88		mpA2Port = "wbMpA2p"
89		mpA2Edge = "wbMpA2e"
90		mpA2IP   = "10.101.2.1"
91		// Edge B uplinks.
92		mpB1Port = "wbMpB1p"
93		mpB1Edge = "wbMpB1e"
94		mpB1IP   = "10.101.1.2"
95		mpB2Port = "wbMpB2p"
96		mpB2Edge = "wbMpB2e"
97		mpB2IP   = "10.101.2.2"
98	
99		// Inner overlay: the concentrator serves 10.10.0.2 (reused concInner); each edge owns
100		// its own inner /32 so the concentrator's per-peer allowed_ips crypto-routes replies
101		// to the correct peer. edgeInner (10.10.0.1) is edge A; edge B is a fresh address.
102		mpEdgeAInner = edgeInner // 10.10.0.1
103		mpEdgeBInner = "10.10.0.3"
104	
105		// Concentrator path names (the metrics `path` label) — shared by both peers' per-path
106		// series, since both peers arrive on the concentrator's same two uplink sockets.
107		mpWan1 = "wan1"
108		mpWan2 = "wan2"
109	
110		// The two edges' configured peer names on the concentrator. edge A is configured FIRST,
111		// so it binds as the unnamed primary (see the per-peer-label note above); edge B keeps
112		// its configured name as its metric label.
113		mpPeerAConfigName = "edge-alpha"
114		mpPeerBConfigName = "edge-beta"
115		mpPeerALabel      = "" // edge A surfaces UNNAMED (primary override), not "edge-alpha"
116		mpPeerBLabel      = mpPeerBConfigName
117	
118		// Per-uplink netem delay (ms). Distinct across a bond so frames reorder and the
119		// resequencer is genuinely exercised per peer.
120		mpDelayWan1 = 20
121		mpDelayWan2 = 55
122	
123		// A spare unenrolled source on the wan1 bridge, from which the spoofed flood is sent:
124		// a real address on the fabric that is NOT a configured peer, so its datagrams reach
125		// the listen port but establish no session (the "unbound source" of the threat model).
126		mpFloodSrcIP = "10.101.1.9"
127	
128		// mpMetricsListen is the CONCENTRATOR's /metrics endpoint for this file, on a port no
129		// other e2e file uses (survey the *MetricsListen constants: the current max is 9101).
130		mpMetricsListen = "127.0.0.1:9102"
131		mpMetricsURL    = "http://" + mpMetricsListen + "/metrics"
132	
133		// iperf3 server ports for the two edges' concurrent transfers (a one-shot `-s -1`
134		// server serves ONE client, so concurrent A+B transfers need distinct ports).
135		mpIperfPortA = 5301
136		mpIperfPortB = 5302
137	)
138	
139	// mpBringUpDeadline bounds how long a freshly-started edge daemon may take to complete its
140	// handshake and carry inner traffic. Generous: it must cover binary start, the WG initial
141	// handshake, and the first probe cadence tick on the shared, CPU/PPS-bound netns fixture.
142	const mpBringUpDeadline = 20 * time.Second
143	
144	// mpEdge is one edge namespace: a PID-addressed holder running a wanbond edge daemon over
145	// two uplinks, plus the handles the test needs to drive traffic INTO its namespace and
146	// restart/roam it. It mirrors the base Topology's nsenter discipline but is edge-scoped.
147	type mpEdge struct {
148		t         *testing.T
149		base      *Topology // for startProc (daemon launched via nsenter into this edge's netns)
150		name      string
151		pid       int
152		holder    *exec.Cmd
153		bin       string
154		cfgPath   string
155		inner     string
156		peerLabel string
157		proc      *proc
158	}
159	
160	// TestMultiPeerConcentratorIsolation is the T97 acceptance (`-run MultiPeer` selects it).
161	// It stands up the concentrator + two bonded edges once, then runs the isolation phases in
162	// order against the shared fixture: independent inner streams, one-edge-restart leaves the
163	// other uninterrupted, per-peer /metrics attribution, single-edge NAT-rebind recovery with
164	// the other undisturbed, and a spoofed unbound-source flood that evicts no live peer.
165	func TestMultiPeerConcentratorIsolation(t *testing.T) {
166		mp := setupMultiPeer(t)
167	
168		// (0) Both bonds up: each edge reaches the concentrator's inner address, and the
169		// concentrator's per-peer path_up series read 1 for BOTH peers on BOTH uplinks.
170		if !mp.edgeA.pingUntil(concInner, mpBringUpDeadline) {
171			t.Fatalf("edge A bond never came up\n--- edge A ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", mp.edgeA.proc.log(), mp.conc.log())
172		}
173		if !mp.edgeB.pingUntil(concInner, mpBringUpDeadline) {
174			t.Fatalf("edge B bond never came up\n--- edge B ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", mp.edgeB.proc.log(), mp.conc.log())
175		}
176		for _, pl := range []string{mpPeerALabel, mpPeerBLabel} {
177			for _, wan := range []string{mpWan1, mpWan2} {
178				waitPeerPathUp(t, mpMetricsURL, pl, wan, 1, mpBringUpDeadline)
179			}
180		}

182		t.Run("independent-inner-streams", func(t *testing.T) {
183			// A bulk TCP transfer through EACH tunnel, concurrently. Both completing with
184			// positive throughput proves the two inner streams verify independently: TCP's
185			// checksum means any cross-peer byte corruption would reset the connection or
186			// truncate the transfer, so two positive results are unambiguous no-cross-talk.
187			xa := mp.edgeA.startTransfer(t, concInner, mpIperfPortA, 6)
188			xb := mp.edgeB.startTransfer(t, concInner, mpIperfPortB, 6)
189			if mbps := xa.result(t); mbps <= 0 {
190				t.Fatalf("edge A inner transfer measured non-positive throughput %.2f Mbit/s", mbps)
191			}
192			if mbps := xb.result(t); mbps <= 0 {
193				t.Fatalf("edge B inner transfer measured non-positive throughput %.2f Mbit/s", mbps)
194			}
195	
196			// Per-peer attribution: after both edges pushed traffic, the concentrator's
197			// per-(peer,path) byte counters must be positive for BOTH peers, and the
198			// per-peer resequencer series must exist for both (the two uplinks' distinct
199			// delay reorders frames the connection-scoped resequencer releases). Absolute
200			// counts are report-only (M10/Q12); the asserted invariant is per-peer presence.
201			exp := scrapeMetrics(t, mpMetricsURL)
202			for _, pl := range []string{mpPeerALabel, mpPeerBLabel} {
203				var peerBytes float64
204				for _, wan := range []string{mpWan1, mpWan2} {
205					rx, ok := exp.PeerPathValue(metrics.MetricRxBytes, pl, wan)
206					if !ok {
207						t.Fatalf("no %s{peer=%q,path=%q} series — per-peer attribution missing", metrics.MetricRxBytes, pl, wan)
208					}
209					peerBytes += rx
210				}
211				if peerBytes <= 0 {
212					t.Fatalf("peer %q carried non-positive rx bytes across its uplinks — the concentrator did not attribute its traffic", pl)
213				}
214				if _, ok := exp.PeerValue(metrics.MetricReseqReleased, pl); !ok {
215					t.Fatalf("no %s{peer=%q} resequencer series for a bonded peer", metrics.MetricReseqReleased, pl)
216				}
217				t.Logf("peer %q attributed %.0f rx bytes across wan1+wan2 (report-only)", pl, peerBytes)
218			}
219		})

221		t.Run("edgeA-restart-leaves-edgeB-uninterrupted", func(t *testing.T) {
222			// A background transfer through edge B that SPANS edge A's kill+restart. If the
223			// concentrator's handling of edge A's teardown/re-handshake perturbed edge B's
224			// session, this single TCP connection would reset (result() fails on a non-nil
225			// iperf3 exit); a positive result proves edge B was uninterrupted.
226			xb := mp.edgeB.startTransfer(t, concInner, mpIperfPortB, 12)
227	
228			time.Sleep(1 * time.Second) // let edge B's flow establish and ramp
229			mp.edgeA.stop()             // kill edge A's daemon (its TUN + sessions drop)
230			t.Logf("killed edge A daemon; edge B transfer must survive the outage + edge A's restart")
231			time.Sleep(1 * time.Second) // edge A stays down briefly
232			mp.edgeA.startDaemon(t)     // restart in the SAME namespace; re-handshakes fresh
233	
234			// Edge A recovers: its bond re-establishes and carries inner traffic again.
235			if !mp.edgeA.pingUntil(concInner, mpBringUpDeadline) {
236				t.Fatalf("edge A did not recover after restart\n--- edge A ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", mp.edgeA.proc.log(), mp.conc.log())
237			}
238	
239			// Edge B's transfer completed across the whole window with no reset.
240			if mbps := xb.result(t); mbps <= 0 {
241				t.Fatalf("edge B transfer was interrupted by edge A's restart (non-positive %.2f Mbit/s)", mbps)
242			}
243	
244			// Edge B's per-peer liveness was never torn down (still up on both uplinks).
245			exp := scrapeMetrics(t, mpMetricsURL)
246			for _, wan := range []string{mpWan1, mpWan2} {
247				if v, ok := exp.PeerPathValue(metrics.MetricUp, mpPeerBLabel, wan); !ok || v != 1 {
248					t.Fatalf("edge B %s{peer=%q,path=%q} = %v (ok=%v), want 1 — edge B liveness was disturbed by edge A's restart", metrics.MetricUp, mpPeerBLabel, wan, v, ok)
249				}
250			}
251		})

253		t.Run("per-peer-metrics-attribute-to-correct-edge", func(t *testing.T) {
254			// Drive a short, per-edge amount of inner traffic, then assert the concentrator's
255			// per-peer series carry BOTH peer labels with independent counters. This is the
256			// "attribute traffic to the correct edge" acceptance: the two peers surface as two
257			// distinct label sets, each with its own path/FEC/resequencer view.
258			if mbps := mp.edgeA.iperf3Mbps(t, concInner, mpIperfPortA, 3); mbps <= 0 {
259				t.Fatalf("edge A transfer non-positive %.2f Mbit/s", mbps)
260			}
261			if mbps := mp.edgeB.iperf3Mbps(t, concInner, mpIperfPortB, 3); mbps <= 0 {
262				t.Fatalf("edge B transfer non-positive %.2f Mbit/s", mbps)
263			}
264	
265			exp := scrapeMetrics(t, mpMetricsURL)
266			// Both peers present with a per-peer FEC series (T94 registers per-peer FEC/reseq
267			// on a multi-peer Source), attributing to two DISTINCT labels.
268			for _, pl := range []string{mpPeerALabel, mpPeerBLabel} {
269				if _, ok := exp.PeerValue(metrics.MetricFECData, pl); !ok {
270					t.Fatalf("no %s{peer=%q} series — the concentrator is not exposing per-peer FEC for both edges", metrics.MetricFECData, pl)
271				}
272				var txTotal float64
273				for _, wan := range []string{mpWan1, mpWan2} {
274					tx, ok := exp.PeerPathValue(metrics.MetricTxBytes, pl, wan)
275					if !ok {
276						t.Fatalf("no %s{peer=%q,path=%q} — per-peer tx attribution missing", metrics.MetricTxBytes, pl, wan)
277					}
278					txTotal += tx
279				}
280				if txTotal <= 0 {
281					t.Fatalf("peer %q has non-positive tx bytes — the concentrator did not attribute its return traffic", pl)
282				}
283			}
284			if mpPeerALabel == mpPeerBLabel {
285				t.Fatal("the two peers must surface under DISTINCT metric labels")
286			}
287		})

289		t.Run("edgeA-nat-rebind-recovers-edgeB-unaffected", func(t *testing.T) {
290			// Move edge A's wan1 uplink to a fresh source address (a NAT rebind / CGNAT churn),
291			// while a background transfer through edge B runs. Edge A's device-bound socket
292			// keeps sending from the new source; the concentrator re-learns the endpoint from
293			// the next authenticated probe (T37). Edge B must be undisturbed throughout.
294			xb := mp.edgeB.startTransfer(t, concInner, mpIperfPortB, 10)
295			time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
296	
297			newIP := reHost(mpA1IP, 121)
298			t.Logf("edge A NAT-rebind: wan1 source %s -> %s (mid-flow)", mpA1IP, newIP)
299			mp.edgeA.readdressUplink(mpA1Edge, newIP)
300	
301			// Prove the RE-BOUND wan1 itself recovered (not merely that edge A survived on
302			// wan2): once wan1 is roamed, bring edge A's wan2 DOWN so only the re-bound wan1
303			// can carry traffic — a successful ping then means the concentrator re-learned the
304			// roamed wan1 endpoint. Mirrors the T16 roaming test's unambiguous proof.
305			mp.edgeA.setUplink(mpA2Edge, false)
306			if !mp.edgeA.pingUntil(concInner, time.Duration(P1RecoverySeconds)*time.Second+3*time.Second) {
307				t.Fatalf("edge A wan1 did not recover on its new source %s (wan2 down)\n--- edge A ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", newIP, mp.edgeA.proc.log(), mp.conc.log())
308			}
309			mp.edgeA.setUplink(mpA2Edge, true) // restore edge A's second uplink
310	
311			// Edge B was unaffected: its transfer completed with no reset.
312			if mbps := xb.result(t); mbps <= 0 {
313				t.Fatalf("edge B transfer was disturbed by edge A's NAT-rebind (non-positive %.2f Mbit/s)", mbps)
314			}
315		})

317		t.Run("spoofed-unbound-source-flood-evicts-no-live-peer", func(t *testing.T) {
318			// Blast garbage UDP at the concentrator's wan1 listen socket from an unenrolled
319			// source on the fabric (no established session). The threat model (M10/Q12): this
320			// may degrade BOOTSTRAP latency for a NEW peer (report-only — not asserted here,
321			// as there is no fresh bootstrapping peer in this fixture), but it must NOT evict a
322			// LIVE peer or corrupt a cross-peer stream.
323			const floodPackets = 3000
324			floodUnboundSource(t, mpFloodSrcIP, mpConc1EP, floodPackets)
325			t.Logf("sent %d spoofed unbound-source datagrams at %s (bootstrap-latency impact is report-only)", floodPackets, mpConc1EP)
326	
327			// Neither live peer was evicted: both still carry inner traffic, and both peers'
328			// per-uplink liveness still reads up.
329			if !mp.edgeA.pingUntil(concInner, mpBringUpDeadline) {
330				t.Fatalf("edge A evicted by the unbound-source flood\n--- conc ---\n%s", mp.conc.log())
331			}
332			if !mp.edgeB.pingUntil(concInner, mpBringUpDeadline) {
333				t.Fatalf("edge B evicted by the unbound-source flood\n--- conc ---\n%s", mp.conc.log())
334			}
335			exp := scrapeMetrics(t, mpMetricsURL)
336			for _, pl := range []string{mpPeerALabel, mpPeerBLabel} {
337				for _, wan := range []string{mpWan1, mpWan2} {
338					if v, ok := exp.PeerPathValue(metrics.MetricUp, pl, wan); !ok || v != 1 {
339						t.Fatalf("peer %q %s{path=%q} = %v (ok=%v) after the flood, want 1 — a live peer was evicted", pl, metrics.MetricUp, wan, v, ok)
340					}
341				}
342			}
343	
344			// Cross-peer streams remain intact end to end: a fresh transfer through each
345			// tunnel still completes positively.
346			if mbps := mp.edgeA.iperf3Mbps(t, concInner, mpIperfPortA, 3); mbps <= 0 {
347				t.Fatalf("edge A cross-peer stream corrupted after the flood (non-positive %.2f Mbit/s)", mbps)
348			}
349			if mbps := mp.edgeB.iperf3Mbps(t, concInner, mpIperfPortB, 3); mbps <= 0 {
350				t.Fatalf("edge B cross-peer stream corrupted after the flood (non-positive %.2f Mbit/s)", mbps)
351			}
352		})

355	// setupMultiPeer builds the whole fixture: the two WAN bridges in the base namespace, the
356	// concentrator daemon (two uplinks, two peers) in the base namespace, and two bonded edge
357	// daemons each in its own PID-addressed holder namespace. It addresses every TUN and
358	// returns once both bonds' TUNs exist. All teardown is registered via t.Cleanup.
359	func setupMultiPeer(t *testing.T) *multiPeerFixture {
360		t.Helper()
361		bin := buildWanbond(t)
362	
363		base := &Topology{t: t}
364		base.run("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
365	
366		// Idempotent pre-delete of the fixed-name bridges/ports (a prior test's teardown can
367		// race the kernel reap in the reused base namespace), then build the two WAN bridges.
368		for _, dev := range []string{mpA1Port, mpA2Port, mpB1Port, mpB2Port, mpBr1, mpBr2} {
369			_ = base.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", dev)
370		}
371		for _, br := range []struct{ name, addr string }{{mpBr1, mpConc1IP}, {mpBr2, mpConc2IP}} {
372			base.run("ip", "link", "add", br.name, "type", "bridge", "stp_state", "0", "forward_delay", "0")
373			base.run("ip", "addr", "add", br.addr+"/24", "dev", br.name)
374			base.run("ip", "link", "set", br.name, "up")
375		}
376		// Spare unenrolled source on the wan1 bridge for the spoofed flood.
377		base.run("ip", "addr", "add", mpFloodSrcIP+"/24", "dev", mpBr1)
378		t.Cleanup(func() {
379			for _, dev := range []string{mpA1Port, mpA2Port, mpB1Port, mpB2Port, mpBr1, mpBr2} {
380				_ = base.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", dev)
381			}
382		})
383	
384		// WireGuard identities: one concentrator keypair with two peers; one keypair per edge.
385		// Each edge's outer-control PSK (its top-level psk) is ALSO its per-peer psk on the
386		// concentrator (T81 multi-peer: present + pairwise-distinct). The concentrator's own
387		// top-level psk is unused for demux in multi-peer mode but must be a valid key.
388		concPriv, concPub := genKey(t)
389		edgeAPriv, edgeAPub := genKey(t)
390		edgeBPriv, edgeBPub := genKey(t)
391		pskA := randKey(t)
392		pskB := randKey(t)
393		topPSK := randKey(t)
394	
395		dir := t.TempDir()
396		concCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "conc.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
397	psk = "%s"
398	
399	[[paths]]
400	name = "%s"
401	source_addr = "%s"
402	
403	[[paths]]
404	name = "%s"
405	source_addr = "%s"
406	
407	[metrics]
408	listen = "%s"
409	
410	[wireguard]
411	private_key = "%s"
412	listen_port = %d
413	
414	[[wireguard.peers]]
415	public_key = "%s"
416	name = "%s"
417	psk = "%s"
418	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
419	
420	[[wireguard.peers]]
421	public_key = "%s"
422	name = "%s"
423	psk = "%s"
424	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
425	
426	[log]
427	level = "info"
428	`, topPSK,
429			mpWan1, mpConc1IP,
430			mpWan2, mpConc2IP,
431			mpMetricsListen,
432			concPriv, listenPort,
433			edgeAPub, mpPeerAConfigName, pskA, mpEdgeAInner,
434			edgeBPub, mpPeerBConfigName, pskB, mpEdgeBInner))
435	
436		edgeACfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "edgeA.toml"), mpEdgeConfig(pskA, edgeAPriv, concPub, mpA1IP, mpA2IP))
437		edgeBCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "edgeB.toml"), mpEdgeConfig(pskB, edgeBPriv, concPub, mpB1IP, mpB2IP))
438	
439		// Concentrator first (it must be listening before the edges initiate), in the base
440		// namespace so its /metrics endpoint is on the test's own 127.0.0.1.
441		conc := base.startProc(t, "concentrator", bin, "--config", concCfg)
442		if !base.waitLink(tunDev, false, 5*time.Second) {
443			t.Fatalf("concentrator %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, conc.log())
444		}
445		base.run("ip", "addr", "add", concInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
446		base.run("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
447	
448		// Edge A and edge B, each in its own namespace with two uplinks.
449		edgeA := newMPEdge(t, base, bin, "edgeA", edgeACfg, mpEdgeAInner, mpPeerALabel,
450			[]mpUplink{{mpA1Port, mpA1Edge, mpA1IP, mpDelayWan1}, {mpA2Port, mpA2Edge, mpA2IP, mpDelayWan2}})
451		edgeB := newMPEdge(t, base, bin, "edgeB", edgeBCfg, mpEdgeBInner, mpPeerBLabel,
452			[]mpUplink{{mpB1Port, mpB1Edge, mpB1IP, mpDelayWan1}, {mpB2Port, mpB2Edge, mpB2IP, mpDelayWan2}})
453	
454		return &multiPeerFixture{t: t, base: base, conc: conc, edgeA: edgeA, edgeB: edgeB}
455	}

457	// multiPeerFixture is the assembled fixture handed to the test phases.
458	type multiPeerFixture struct {
459		t     *testing.T
460		base  *Topology
461		conc  *proc
462		edgeA *mpEdge
463		edgeB *mpEdge
464	}
465	
466	// mpUplink describes one edge uplink: the bridge-side veth end (on the WAN bridge), the
467	// in-namespace veth end (the source-bound interface, netem-shaped), its address, and the
468	// egress netem delay in ms.
469	type mpUplink struct {
470		port    string
471		edge    string
472		ip      string
473		delayMs int
474	}
475	
476	// newMPEdge opens a fresh network namespace, wires each uplink veth pair from the matching
477	// WAN bridge into it (addressed + netem-shaped), then starts and addresses the edge daemon.
478	func newMPEdge(t *testing.T, base *Topology, bin, name, cfgPath, inner, peerLabel string, uplinks []mpUplink) *mpEdge {
479		t.Helper()
480		holder := exec.Command("unshare", "-n", "sleep", "600")
481		if err := holder.Start(); err != nil {
482			t.Fatalf("start %s netns holder: %v", name, err)
483		}
484		pid := holder.Process.Pid
485		t.Cleanup(func() {
486			_ = holder.Process.Kill()
487			_, _ = holder.Process.Wait()
488		})
489	
490		e := &mpEdge{t: t, base: base, name: name, pid: pid, holder: holder, bin: bin, cfgPath: cfgPath, inner: inner, peerLabel: peerLabel}
491		(&Topology{t: t, pid: pid}).waitForNetns()
492		e.run("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
493	
494		for _, u := range uplinks {
495			bridge := mpBridgeForIP(u.ip)
496			base.run("ip", "link", "add", u.port, "type", "veth", "peer", "name", u.edge)
497			base.run("ip", "link", "set", u.edge, "netns", strconv.Itoa(pid))
498			base.run("ip", "link", "set", u.port, "master", bridge)
499			base.run("ip", "link", "set", u.port, "up")
500	
501			// The netns move is async w.r.t. in-namespace addressing: the device becoming
502			// visible does NOT guarantee the immediately-following `addr add` succeeds (D33).
503			// Retry the ACTUAL addressing op (bounded) so setup waits for genuine usability.
504			deadline := time.Now().Add(5 * time.Second)
505			for {
506				err := e.tryRun("ip", "addr", "add", u.ip+"/24", "dev", u.edge)
507				if err == nil {
508					break
509				}
510				if time.Now().After(deadline) {
511					t.Fatalf("%s: could not add %s/24 to %q in netns %d within 5s: %v", name, u.ip, u.edge, pid, err)
512				}
513				time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
514			}
515			e.run("ip", "link", "set", u.edge, "up")
516			// Distinct per-uplink egress delay so a bonded edge's frames reorder.
517			e.run("tc", "qdisc", "add", "dev", u.edge, "root", "netem", "delay", strconv.Itoa(u.delayMs)+"ms")
518		}
519	
520		e.startDaemon(t)
521		return e
522	}
523	
524	// mpBridgeForIP returns the WAN bridge an uplink address belongs to (by its /24).
525	func mpBridgeForIP(ip string) string {
526		if strings.HasPrefix(ip, "10.101.1.") {
527			return mpBr1
528		}
529		return mpBr2
530	}

532	// mpEdgeConfig renders an edge config bonded across two uplinks to the concentrator's two
533	// WAN addresses. Both edges share the concentrator's public key and endpoint; only the psk,
534	// private key, and per-uplink source addresses differ.
535	func mpEdgeConfig(psk, edgePriv, concPub, up1Src, up2Src string) string {
536		return fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
537	psk = "%s"
538	
539	[[paths]]
540	name = "%s"
541	source_addr = "%s"
542	dest_addr = "%s"
543	
544	[[paths]]
545	name = "%s"
546	source_addr = "%s"
547	dest_addr = "%s"
548	
549	[wireguard]
550	private_key = "%s"
551	
552	[[wireguard.peers]]
553	public_key = "%s"
554	endpoint = "%s"
555	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
556	
557	[log]
558	level = "info"
559	`, psk,
560			mpWan1, up1Src, mpConc1EP,
561			mpWan2, up2Src, mpConc2EP,
562			edgePriv,
563			concPub, mpConc1EP, concInner)
564	}

566	// startDaemon (re)starts the edge daemon inside this edge's namespace and addresses its
567	// TUN. Used at initial bring-up and by restart (after stop()).
568	func (e *mpEdge) startDaemon(t *testing.T) {
569		t.Helper()
570		e.proc = e.base.startProc(t, e.name, "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(e.pid), "-n", e.bin, "--config", e.cfgPath)
571		if !e.waitTUN(5 * time.Second) {
572			t.Fatalf("%s %s never appeared\n%s", e.name, tunDev, e.proc.log())
573		}
574		e.run("ip", "addr", "add", e.inner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
575		e.run("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
576	}
577	
578	// stop kills the edge daemon and waits for it to exit (its TUN + sessions drop with it).
579	// The original startProc cleanup still fires at test end and tolerates the double-kill.
580	func (e *mpEdge) stop() {
581		if e.proc == nil || e.proc.cmd.Process == nil {
582			return
583		}
584		_ = e.proc.cmd.Process.Kill()
585		_, _ = e.proc.cmd.Process.Wait()
586	}
587	
588	// waitTUN polls until this edge's wanbond0 exists in its namespace.
589	func (e *mpEdge) waitTUN(d time.Duration) bool {
590		deadline := time.Now().Add(d)
591		for time.Now().Before(deadline) {
592			if e.tryRun("ip", "link", "show", tunDev) == nil {
593				return true
594			}
595			time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
596		}
597		return false
598	}
599	
600	// readdressUplink flushes and re-adds an uplink's edge-side address, simulating a NAT
601	// rebind / edge public-IP move (its netem qdisc is preserved — flush touches addresses,
602	// not qdiscs).
603	func (e *mpEdge) readdressUplink(edgeVeth, newIP string) {
604		e.run("ip", "addr", "flush", "dev", edgeVeth)
605		e.run("ip", "addr", "add", newIP+"/24", "dev", edgeVeth)
606		e.run("ip", "link", "set", edgeVeth, "up")
607	}
608	
609	// setUplink brings an uplink's edge-side veth up or down (to isolate a single path).
610	func (e *mpEdge) setUplink(edgeVeth string, up bool) {
611		state := "down"
612		if up {
613			state = "up"
614		}
615		e.run("ip", "link", "set", edgeVeth, state)
616	}
617	
618	// pingUntil pings ip from THIS edge's namespace until it answers or d elapses.
619	func (e *mpEdge) pingUntil(ip string, d time.Duration) bool {
620		deadline := time.Now().Add(d)
621		for time.Now().Before(deadline) {
622			if e.tryRun("ping", "-c", "1", "-W", "1", ip) == nil {
623				return true
624			}
625			time.Sleep(200 * time.Millisecond)
626		}
627		return false
628	}
629	
630	// iperf3Mbps runs a one-shot iperf3 TCP transfer from this edge to serverIP (the
631	// concentrator inner address, served in the base namespace) for secs seconds and returns
632	// the sender throughput in Mbit/s.
633	func (e *mpEdge) iperf3Mbps(t *testing.T, serverIP string, port, secs int) float64 {
634		t.Helper()
635		startBaseIperfServer(t, e.base, serverIP, port)
636		out := e.runOut("iperf3", "-c", serverIP, "-p", strconv.Itoa(port), "-t", strconv.Itoa(secs), "-J")
637		return parseIperfMbps(t, out)
638	}
639	
640	// startTransfer starts a background iperf3 transfer from this edge to serverIP, returning
641	// immediately; result() awaits it. Used for flows that must SPAN a mid-run disruption.
642	func (e *mpEdge) startTransfer(t *testing.T, serverIP string, port, secs int) *transfer {
643		t.Helper()
644		startBaseIperfServer(t, e.base, serverIP, port)
645		cmd := exec.Command("nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(e.pid), "-n", "iperf3", "-c", serverIP, "-p", strconv.Itoa(port), "-t", strconv.Itoa(secs), "-J")
646		out := &lockedBuffer{}
647		cmd.Stdout, cmd.Stderr = out, out
648		if err := cmd.Start(); err != nil {
649			t.Fatalf("%s: start iperf3 client: %v", e.name, err)
650		}
651		return &transfer{cmd: cmd, out: out}
652	}
653	
654	// run executes a command inside this edge's namespace, failing the test on error.
655	func (e *mpEdge) run(args ...string) {
656		e.t.Helper()
657		full := append([]string{"-t", strconv.Itoa(e.pid), "-n"}, args...)
658		if out, err := exec.Command("nsenter", full...).CombinedOutput(); err != nil {
659			e.t.Fatalf("%s: nsenter %s: %v\n%s", e.name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
660		}
661	}
662	
663	// tryRun executes a command inside this edge's namespace, returning the error (no fatal).
664	func (e *mpEdge) tryRun(args ...string) error {
665		full := append([]string{"-t", strconv.Itoa(e.pid), "-n"}, args...)
666		return exec.Command("nsenter", full...).Run()
667	}
668	
669	// runOut executes a command inside this edge's namespace and returns its combined output,
670	// failing the test on error.
671	func (e *mpEdge) runOut(args ...string) string {
672		e.t.Helper()
673		full := append([]string{"-t", strconv.Itoa(e.pid), "-n"}, args...)
674		out, err := exec.Command("nsenter", full...).CombinedOutput()
675		if err != nil {
676			e.t.Fatalf("%s: nsenter %s: %v\n%s", e.name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
677		}
678		return string(out)
679	}
680	
681	// startBaseIperfServer starts a one-shot iperf3 server bound to serverIP:port in the base
682	// (concentrator) namespace and returns once it reaches LISTEN.
683	func startBaseIperfServer(t *testing.T, base *Topology, serverIP string, port int) {
684		t.Helper()
685		base.startProc(t, "iperf3-server", "iperf3", "-s", "-1", "-B", serverIP, "-p", strconv.Itoa(port))
686		baseWaitIperfListen(t, port)
687	}
688	
689	// baseWaitIperfListen polls the base namespace for a TCP LISTEN socket on port (never
690	// connecting — the server is one-shot), failing the test at iperfListenTimeout.
691	func baseWaitIperfListen(t *testing.T, port int) {
692		t.Helper()
693		suffix := ":" + strconv.Itoa(port)
694		deadline := time.Now().Add(iperfListenTimeout)
695		for time.Now().Before(deadline) {
696			out, err := exec.Command("ss", "-ltn").CombinedOutput()
697			if err == nil {
698				for _, line := range strings.Split(string(out), "\n") {
699					fields := strings.Fields(line)
700					if len(fields) >= 4 && strings.HasSuffix(fields[3], suffix) {
701						return
702					}
703				}
704			}
705			time.Sleep(20 * time.Millisecond)
706		}
707		t.Fatalf("base iperf3 server never reached LISTEN on port %d within %s", port, iperfListenTimeout)
708	}
709	
710	// parseIperfMbps extracts the sender throughput (Mbit/s) from iperf3 -J output.
711	func parseIperfMbps(t *testing.T, out string) float64 {
712		t.Helper()
713		var r struct {
714			End struct {
715				SumSent struct {
716					BitsPerSecond float64 `json:"bits_per_second"`
717				} `json:"sum_sent"`
718			} `json:"end"`
719		}
720		if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(out), &r); err != nil {
721			t.Fatalf("parse iperf3 json: %v\n%s", err, out)
722		}
723		return r.End.SumSent.BitsPerSecond / 1e6
724	}
725	
726	// waitPeerPathUp polls url's /metrics until the PER-PEER wanbond_path_up series for the
727	// given (peer,path) reads want, or fails at deadline. A mid-poll scrape error is tolerated
728	// (transient). It is the T94 per-peer analogue of waitPathUp.
729	func waitPeerPathUp(t *testing.T, url, peer, path string, want float64, deadline time.Duration) {
730		t.Helper()
731		end := time.Now().Add(deadline)
732		var lastErr error
733		for time.Now().Before(end) {
734			ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 1*time.Second)
735			exp, err := metrics.Fetch(ctx, http.DefaultClient, url)
736			cancel()
737			if err != nil {
738				lastErr = err
739			} else if v, ok := exp.PeerPathValue(metrics.MetricUp, peer, path); ok && v == want {
740				return
741			}
742			time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
743		}
744		t.Fatalf("%s{peer=%q,path=%q} never reached %v within %s (last scrape error: %v)", metrics.MetricUp, peer, path, want, deadline, lastErr)
745	}
746	
747	// floodUnboundSource sends count garbage UDP datagrams to dstAddr from localAddr — a real,
748	// unenrolled source on the fabric, so the datagrams reach the listen port but establish no
749	// session (the "spoofed unbound source" of the threat model). It runs in the base namespace
750	// (the test process's namespace, where both localAddr and the concentrator listen).
751	func floodUnboundSource(t *testing.T, localAddr, dstAddr string, count int) {
752		t.Helper()
753		la, err := net.ResolveUDPAddr("udp", localAddr+":0")
754		if err != nil {
755			t.Fatalf("resolve flood source %s: %v", localAddr, err)
756		}
757		ra, err := net.ResolveUDPAddr("udp", dstAddr)
758		if err != nil {
759			t.Fatalf("resolve flood dest %s: %v", dstAddr, err)
760		}
761		conn, err := net.DialUDP("udp", la, ra)
762		if err != nil {
763			t.Fatalf("dial flood socket %s->%s: %v", localAddr, dstAddr, err)
764		}
765		defer func() { _ = conn.Close() }()
766		junk := make([]byte, 128)
767		if _, err := rand.Read(junk); err != nil {
768			t.Fatalf("read flood payload: %v", err)
769		}
770		for i := 0; i < count; i++ {
771			if _, err := conn.Write(junk); err != nil {
772				// A transient ENOBUFS under a tight loop is not a test failure — the flood is a
773				// best-effort saturation, not an exact packet count.
774				break
775			}
776		}
777	}

input.description: Inspect Topology run/tryRun pid handling and hub_failover base pattern

content.text:
All evidence gathered. Compiling the verdict.

**Evidence assembled:**

- **(a) Gates:** `go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e`, `go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e`, and `go test ./...` all pass in the worktree under `nix develop`.
- **(b) Port 9102 unique:** survey of every `*MetricsListen` constant: 9095 (p2), 9096 (pacing, p3), 9097 (p4), 9098 (t60), 9099 (hub_failover), 9100 (t104), 9101 (i2/session_established). 9102 is free. (Side finding: pacing and p3 both use 9096 — pre-existing, filed as a defect.)
- **(c) Label expectation grounded in production code:** `internal/device/device.go:290-316` binds the FIRST identity via `NewMultipath` (unnamed) and only `ids[1:]` via `AddConcentratorPeer(id.Name, ...)` — so edge-alpha (first configured) surfaces as `peer=""` and edge-beta keeps its name, exactly as `mpPeerALabel`/`mpPeerBLabel` encode. The empty-peer-label scrape round-trip (`PeerPathValue(name, "", path)` over a real HTTP fetch) is already proven green by `TestExpositionTwoPeerSeries` in `internal/metrics/metrics_test.go:336-372`, and `internal/bind/concentrator_peer_test.go:106` pins `BoundPeerNames() == ["", "beta"]`. The multi-peer registration path adds the `peer` label to path series (`metrics.go:261-265`), so `waitPeerPathUp` on `(peer,path)` is well-formed. A wrong-label spurious hardware failure is ruled out by three independent unit-level proofs.
- **(d) Report-only discipline:** absolute byte counts are `t.Logf`-reported only; assertions are presence + positivity of per-peer series, and the isolation properties genuinely `Fatalf`: cross-edge integrity via two concurrent TCP transfers (a reset/truncation fails `transfer.result`, which fails on any non-nil iperf3 exit — `roaming_test.go:124-128`), edge-B interruption via a transfer *spanning* edge A's kill+restart, and eviction via ping + `wanbond_path_up{peer,path}==1` for both peers on both uplinks.
- **(e) Flood fidelity:** the flood source is a real, unenrolled address on the wan1 bridge; datagrams reach the listen socket and exercise the unbound-source demux path. Assertions are eviction-focused (both peers still ping, per-(peer,path) up==1, fresh transfers complete), not merely no-crash; bootstrap-latency degradation is correctly left report-only (no bootstrapping peer exists in the fixture). `DialUDP` failing fatals the test, so the flood cannot silently become a no-op.
- **(f) Untouched fixture:** the diff is exactly one added file (`test/e2e/multipeer_test.go`, +777); `netns.go`/`thresholds.go` and all existing e2e files are byte-identical to base.
- **Config surface grounded:** every rendered TOML key (`role`, per-peer `name`/`psk`/`allowed_ips`, `listen_port`, `source_addr`/`dest_addr`, `[metrics] listen`) exists in `internal/config/config.go`; per-peer PSKs are pairwise-distinct random keys, satisfying the T81 multi-peer rule. Shared helpers (`transfer`, `reHost`, `scrapeMetrics`, `startProc`, D33-style retry on in-netns addressing) are used per their contracts; the base-`Topology{t}` / PID-holder pattern mirrors `hub_failover_test.go:244,364`.

**Hardware-only residue (cannot be validated here):** actual two-edge bring-up within the 20s `mpBringUpDeadline` on the CPU/PPS-bound hosts; NAT-rebind re-learn within `P1RecoverySeconds+3s` under per-peer probers; the 600s holder lifetime comfortably bounding total runtime on the slow amd64 host; iperf3 concurrency behavior on shaped veths.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T97 round 1 (diff a146fde..6df7dbc, single added file test/e2e/multipeer_test.go)
- **Achieved:** verdict approve; 0 criticisms / 0 questions; 1 out-of-scope defect filed (pre-existing 9096 metrics-port collision)
- **Discovered:** vet/build (-tags e2e) + full unit suite green; peer=""/named-label expectation verified against device.go wiring and three unit-level proofs; port 9102 confirmed unique; isolation assertions genuinely fatal while absolute numbers stay report-only; fixture files untouched
- **Issues:** the privileged netns tier itself is hardware-deferred — bring-up timing, NAT-rebind recovery window, and the 600s holder bound are validated only by the o3/llm-ubuntu-0 run
